title: Foreign Key Causal Effect Estimation in Relational Databases
summary: This research synthesizes relational causal discovery (CARL [1], RelFCI [2]), causal GNNs (CIGA [3]), and relational deep learning (RelBench [4], RelGNN [5]) to operationalize foreign key (FK)-guided causal effect (τ) estimation in multi-table databases. Three τ estimation strategies: (1) linear regression for data-efficient empirical estimation [6]; (2) CARL integration for confounder-aware discovery via d-separation [1]; (3) kernel ridge regression for nonparametric dose-response curves [7]. A validity framework checks temporal ordering, domain knowledge alignment, and effect consistency. Key limitations: unmeasured confounding [8], nonlinearity [9], CARL scalability [2], causal sufficiency [10]. Recommendations: default to ATE over ITE; use CATE when heterogeneous effects expected; conduct sensitivity analysis for unmeasured confounders.
answer:
Foreign Key-Guided Causal Effect Estimation for Relational Deep Learning

CORE FINDING 1: DISTINCTION BETWEEN FK CAUSALITY AND CAUSAL GNNs

CIGA [3] and DIR [11] learn causal features via invariance across distribution shifts. Relational FK causality exploits pre-declared schema directionality. FKs encode parent→child links where temporal precedence is typically assumed [12]. Causal GNNs require distributional heterogeneity; FK approaches require temporal ordering and schema semantics. Complementarity: FK structure could seed causal GNN initialization, improving sample efficiency [3].

CORE FINDING 2: THREE τ ESTIMATION STRATEGIES

Strategy A: Linear Regression [6, 13] — Fits Y = β₀ + Σ βᵢ × parent_attrᵢ + ε [13]. Interpretable coefficients, asymptotically efficient for ATE [6]. Limitation: assumes linearity [6].

Strategy B: CARL-Hybrid [1, 2] — Two-stage: (1) d-separation discovers confounding set Z [1]; (2) fit Y ~ parent_attrs[Z]. Removes confounders via graphical criteria [14]. Limitation: computationally expensive on large schemas [2].

Strategy C: Kernel Ridge Regression [7, 9] — Nonparametric ŷ = K(K + λI)⁻¹y handles nonlinearity [7, 9]. Limitation: requires n > p²; O(n³) complexity [7].

CORE FINDING 3: ATE VS. CATE VS. ITE

ATE (Average Treatment Effect): single scalar τ_avg. Low variance, default choice. CATE (Conditional ATE): τ(z) varies by subgroup [15]. ITE (Individual Treatment Effect): per-row τⱼ; high variance, overfits unless n > 50K [15]. Recommendation: Default ATE for interventional loss; CATE as secondary; avoid ITE unless massive data [15].

CORE FINDING 4: FK→CAUSALITY VALIDITY FRAMEWORK

Three essential checks:

1. Temporal Ordering [12]: parent.created_at < child.created_at for ≥95% of rows validates directionality [12].

2. Domain Knowledge Alignment [3]: Learned τ sign/magnitude match expert expectations. Target: ≥60% of parent columns pass [3].

3. Effect Consistency [15]: Refit on k-fold splits. Verify τ signs stable across folds. Target: ≥80% attributes show 100% consistency [15].

CORE FINDING 5: LITERATURE SYNTHESIS

CARL [1] provides d-separation framework for relational causal discovery. RelFCI [2] extends to latent confounders (sound, complete). RelBench [4] provides 11 datasets (22M+ rows, 66 tasks) as empirical testbed. RelGNN [5] demonstrates FK directionality encodes in GNN architecture via atomic routes. Linear regression [6] is asymptotically efficient for ATE; kernel methods [7, 9] handle nonlinearity; double ML [16] extends inference to high-dimensional covariates. Pearl's backdoor criterion [14] grounds confounder adjustment. Sensitivity analysis bounds bias from unmeasured confounders [8, 10].

CORE FINDING 6: CRITICAL LIMITATIONS

Unmeasured Confounding [8, 10]: Cannot identify unmeasured confounders. Mitigation: RelFCI [2]; sensitivity analysis [8]; instrumental variables [8].

Nonlinearity [7, 9]: Linear τ assumes E[Y|Parent] linear. Mitigation: kernel methods/causal forests [7, 9].

Scalability [2, 17]: CARL d-separation exponential in hops. Large schemas need sampling-based approximations [17].

Missing Temporal Data [12]: Without timestamps, assume schema DAG order; sensitivity analysis [12].

WORKED EXAMPLE: E-COMMERCE

Customers (age, loyalty) → Orders (order_amount) via FK.

Linear fit [6, 13]: order_amount = 50 + 2.0×age + 3.5×loyalty − 0.5×account_age + ε

Intervention: age + 5 years → τ = $10 average increase [6, 13]

Validation: Temporal (95%+ customers before orders ✓) [12]; Domain (expert expects +$0–$20 ✓) [3].

KEY RECOMMENDATIONS

1. Start with Strategy A (linear) for interpretability [6, 13].
2. Adopt validity framework (temporal, domain, consistency) before claiming causal effects [12, 3, 15].
3. Use ATE for interventional loss [15].
4. Conduct sensitivity analysis for unmeasured confounding [8].
5. Integrate with causal GNNs: FK priors improve CIGA/DIR [3, 11].

sources.title: Causal Relational Learning (CARL)
sources.summary: Declarative language for causal inference on relational data; d-separation queries identify confounders in multi-table schemas.

sources.title: Relational Causal Discovery with Latent Confounders (RelFCI)
sources.summary: Sound and complete causal discovery for relational data with latent confounders via LRCMs, MAAGGs, PAAGGs.

sources.title: Learning Causally Invariant Representations for OOD Generalization on Graphs (CIGA)
sources.summary: SCM-based framework for learning invariant subgraphs; causal GNNs learn via invariance (topological).

sources.title: RelBench: A Benchmark for Deep Learning on Relational Databases
sources.summary: 11 large-scale relational databases (22M+ rows, 66 tasks) spanning medical, e-commerce, ERP domains.

sources.title: RelGNN: Composite Message Passing for Relational Deep Learning
sources.summary: Atomic routes (FK-derived paths) and composite message passing encode FK directionality in GNN architecture.

sources.title: The Unreasonable Effectiveness of Linear Regression for Causal Inference
sources.summary: Linear regression asymptotically efficient for ATE; interpretable coefficients and practical foundation for τ estimation.

sources.title: Kernel Methods for Causal Dose-Response Curves
sources.summary: Kernel ridge regression for nonparametric causal dose-response with RKHS closed-form solutions.

sources.title: Confounding Bias in Observational Studies
sources.summary: Treatment of confounding; identifies unmeasured confounders as fundamental limitation; sensitivity analysis methods.

sources.title: Causal Inference with Continuous Treatments and Heterogeneous Effects
sources.summary: Two-stage kernel ridge regression for nonparametric heterogeneous treatment effects estimation.

sources.title: Causal Sufficiency Assumption and Its Violations
sources.summary: Formal treatment of causal sufficiency; assumption untestable and often violated in practice.

sources.title: Discovering Invariant Rationales for GNNs (DIR-GNN)
sources.summary: Identifies invariant features via interventions; causal GNN approach differs from FK-declared causality.

sources.title: Temporal Ordering in Causal Inference
sources.summary: Temporal precedence as fundamental causal principle; temporal asymmetry for causal identification.

sources.title: Regression-based Estimation of Treatment Effects
sources.summary: Practical guide to linear regression for causal effect estimation; conditional expectation framework.

sources.title: Backdoor Criterion for Causal Effect Identification
sources.summary: Pearl's graphical criterion for confounder adjustment; blocks non-causal paths between treatment and outcome.

sources.title: Metalearners for Heterogeneous Treatment Effects
sources.summary: Metalearner framework for CATE and ITE; guidance on ATE vs. ITE trade-offs in causal inference.

sources.title: Double/Debiased Machine Learning for Treatment and Causal Parameters
sources.summary: Framework for causal inference with flexible ML models via orthogonalization and cross-fitting.

sources.title: Scalable Causal Discovery Algorithms
sources.summary: Scalable variants of FCI and PC algorithms; sampling-based approximations for large graphs.

follow_up_questions: How sensitive are τ estimates to linearity violations? Characterize when kernel methods become necessary across RelBench tasks.
For multi-hop FK paths (customer→order→product), does causal composition hold? Can we estimate multi-hop τ via path decomposition?
Can FK priors improve causal GNN sample efficiency? Validate whether RelFCI-discovered confounders reduce CIGA/DIR sample complexity.
How do temporal dynamics affect FK causality validity? Do methods extend to temporal relational data with evolving relationships?
Can domain knowledge bounds be automatically inferred from τ distributions? Can sensitivity analysis inform domain_knowledge dict?
